content:
You’re close—what’s breaking you right now is:
launching Chrome from PowerShell (the -- parsing error), and
selectors that don’t match the new Copilot UI / wrong entry URL.
Let’s lock this into a “attach-to-debugging-Chrome, single-chat scraper for the new UI” and keep it tight.
1. Start Chrome with remote debugging (PowerShell-safe)
Close all Chrome windows, then in PowerShell run:
Powershell
Copy
& "C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" `
  --remote-debugging-port=9222 `
  --user-data-dir="C:\Users\Sir\AppData\Local\Google\Chrome\User Data"
Key bits:
The & tells PowerShell “run this path as a command”.
The backticks ` just wrap the command across lines; you can also put it all on one line if you want.
Now log into Copilot in that Chrome window and open the chat you care about, e.g.:
https://copilot.microsoft.com/chats/kAfJL9QNyYH1AGesfGatD
Leave that tab open.
2. main_scrape.py (final entrypoint)
Put this at the repo root, replacing your current main_scrape.py:
Python
Copy
import sys
import json

from src.copilot_scraper import scrape_copilot_chat


def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 3:
        print("Usage: python main_scrape.py <chat_url> <output.json>")
        sys.exit(1)

    url = sys.argv[1]
    output_path = sys.argv[2]

    messages = scrape_copilot_chat(url)

    with open(output_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(messages, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

    print(f"Scraped {len(messages)} messages and saved to {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Run it like:
Bash
Copy
python main_scrape.py "https://copilot.microsoft.com/chats/kAfJL9QNyYH1AGesfGatD" output.json
(No --no-headless needed anymore—we’re attaching to your already-open Chrome.)
3. src/copilot_scraper.py (attach-to-debugger, new UI selectors)
Replace your existing src/copilot_scraper.py with this:
Python
Copy
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import List, Dict

from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.chrome.options import Options
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By
from selenium.webdriver.support.ui import WebDriverWait
from selenium.webdriver.support import expected_conditions as EC
import time


# This must match the port you used when starting Chrome
DEBUGGER_ADDRESS = "127.0.0.1:9222"


@contextmanager
def attach_browser():
    """
    Attach to an already-running Chrome that was started with
    --remote-debugging-port=9222.

    We do NOT quit the browser at the end; we just drop the connection.
    """
    opts = Options()
    opts.add_experimental_option("debuggerAddress", DEBUGGER_ADDRESS)

    driver = webdriver.Chrome(options=opts)
    try:
        yield driver
    finally:
        # Don't call driver.quit() here; that would close your real browser.
        # Just let the script end and the connection drop.
        pass


def _wait_for_chat_loaded(driver, timeout: int = 60):
    """
    Wait until at least one conversation turn is present in the DOM.
    The new Copilot UI wraps turns in elements with data-testid or
    the 'group/ai-message-item' class.
    """
    WebDriverWait(driver, timeout).until(
        EC.presence_of_element_located(
            (
                By.CSS_SELECTOR,
                "[data-testid='conversation-turn'], div[class*='group/ai-message-item']",
            )
        )
    )
    # Give it a moment to settle
    time.sleep(2)


def _collect_turn_elements(driver):
    """
    Collect all visible conversation turns in the current chat.
    """
    turns = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, "[data-testid='conversation-turn']")
    if not turns:
        # Fallback for layouts where the class is the main marker
        turns = driver.find_elements(
            By.CSS_SELECTOR, "div[class*='group/ai-message-item']"
        )
    return turns


def _infer_role(el) -> str:
    """
    Infer 'user' vs 'assistant' from class names / attributes.
    This is heuristic but works well with the current Copilot UI.
    """
    cls = (el.get_attribute("class") or "").lower()
    data_testid = (el.get_attribute("data-testid") or "").lower()

    if "user-message" in cls or "group/user-message" in cls or "user" in data_testid:
        return "user"

    # Default to assistant if unsure
    return "assistant"


def scrape_copilot_chat(url: str, timeout: int = 60) -> List[Dict]:
    """
    Attach to the debugging Chrome, open the given chat URL,
    and return a list of {role, content} messages.
    """
    if url.rstrip("/") == "https://copilot.microsoft.com/chats":
        raise ValueError(
            "Pass a specific chat URL like https://copilot.microsoft.com/chats/<id>, "
            "not the history list."
        )

    with attach_browser() as driver:
        print(f"Attaching to browser and opening: {url}")
        driver.get(url)

        _wait_for_chat_loaded(driver, timeout=timeout)

        turns = _collect_turn_elements(driver)
        print(f"Found {len(turns)} conversation turns.")

        messages: List[Dict] = []

        for el in turns:
            text = (el.text or "").strip()
            if not text:
                continue

            role = _infer_role(el)

            messages.append(
                {
                    "role": role,
                    "content": text,
                }
            )

        return messages
If you want to extend this later to “full history harvester”, we can add a second function that:
opens https://copilot.microsoft.com/chats,
clicks each role="option" conversation in the sidebar one by one,
calls scrape_copilot_chat(driver.current_url, ...) internally.
But for now, this gives you a final, working scraper that:
attaches to your signed-in Chrome via remote debugging,
understands the new Copilot UI,
and dumps a clean {role, content} transcript for any specific chat URL you feed it.
If it still times out, paste the current page HTML for one chat and we’ll tune the selectors once more.
